quiz.title: SAT Mathematics: Unit 9 Quiz 11
quiz.subtitle: Two-Way Tables
quiz.subject: SAT Preparation
quiz.topic: Analyzing categorical data in two-way frequency tables

questions.question:
The table below shows the results of a survey regarding preference for a new park design among residents of two neighborhoods.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Design A} & \text{Design B} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Neighborhood 1} & 40 & 60 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Neighborhood 2} & 80 & 20 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 120 & 80 & 200 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If a resident who prefers Design B is selected at random, what is the probability that the resident is from Neighborhood 1?
questions.explanation: This is a conditional probability. The restricted sample space is the total who prefer Design B (80). Within that group, 60 are from Neighborhood 1. The probability is $60/80$, which simplifies to $3/4$.

questions.question:
A group of 150 students was surveyed about their participation in sports and music. The results are shown below.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Plays Sports} & \text{No Sports} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Plays Music} & 35 & 45 & 80 \\ \hline \text{No Music} & 50 & 20 & 70 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 85 & 65 & 150 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

What percentage of the students who do NOT play sports play a musical instrument?
questions.explanation: Identify the total number of students who do not play sports (65). Of these, 45 play music. The percentage is $(45 / 65) \times 100 \approx 69.23\%$.

questions.question:
The table summarizes the inventory of a bookstore by genre and format.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Hardcover} & \text{Paperback} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Fiction} & 120 & 280 & 400 \\ \hline \text{Non-Fiction} & 180 & 120 & 300 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 300 & 400 & 700 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If a book is selected at random, which of the following is the best interpretation of the fraction $\frac{180}{300}$?
options: Probability a book is Non-Fiction and Hardcover.
Probability a Hardcover book is Non-Fiction.
Probability a Non-Fiction book is Hardcover.
Proportion of all books that are Hardcover.
questions.explanation: The denominator (300) represents all hardcover books. The numerator (180) represents non-fiction hardcovers. This fraction calculates the conditional probability that a book is non-fiction given it is already known to be hardcover.

questions.question:
A clinic tested a group of people for a virus. The table below shows the results.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Infected} & \text{Not Infected} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Positive Test} & 48 & 12 & 60 \\ \hline \text{Negative Test} & 2 & 138 & 140 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 50 & 150 & 200 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

What is the probability that a person who tested positive is actually NOT infected?
questions.explanation: Focus exclusively on the 'Positive Test' row (Total = 60). The number of people in this row who are 'Not Infected' is 12. The probability is $12/60 = 1/5$.

questions.question:
A car dealership tracks sales by vehicle type and color.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Sedan} & \text{SUV} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{White} & 15 & 25 & 40 \\ \hline \text{Black} & 20 & 10 & 30 \\ \hline \text{Silver} & 10 & 20 & 30 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 45 & 55 & 100 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If a car is chosen at random, what is the ratio of Black Sedans to all Silver vehicles?
questions.explanation: The number of Black Sedans is 20. The total number of Silver vehicles is 30. The ratio is 20:30, which simplifies to 2:3.

questions.question:
A group of 200 tourists was asked which language they preferred for a guided tour. The table below shows the results categorized by age group.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{English} & \text{Spanish} & \text{French} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Under 40} & 45 & 35 & 20 & 100 \\ \hline \text{40 and Over} & 30 & 45 & 25 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 75 & 80 & 45 & 200 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

What fraction of the tourists who preferred Spanish were in the 'Under 40' age group?
questions.explanation: The total number of tourists who preferred Spanish is 80. Out of these 80 tourists, 35 are in the 'Under 40' group. The fraction is $35/80$, which simplifies to $7/16$.

questions.question:
The table below summarizes the results of a clinical trial for a new allergy medication.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Improved} & \text{No Change} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Medication} & 70 & 30 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Placebo} & 20 & 80 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 90 & 110 & 200 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which of the following is the closest to the probability that a participant who showed 'No Change' was given the actual medication?
questions.explanation: First, identify the total number of participants who showed 'No Change' (110). Among those 110 participants, 30 were in the medication group. The probability is $30/110 \approx 0.2727$, or roughly 0.27.

questions.question:
A school cafeteria surveyed students about their lunch preferences. The data is shown in the two-way table below.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Pizza} & \text{Salad} & \text{Sandwich} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{9th Grade} & 50 & 15 & 35 & 100 \\ \hline \text{10th Grade} & 50 & 25 & 25 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 100 & 40 & 60 & 200 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If a student is selected at random, what is the probability that the student is in the 10th grade OR prefers pizza?
questions.explanation: Using the formula $P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \text{ and } B)$: $(100 + 100 - 50) / 200 = 150/200 = 3/4$.

questions.question:
The table below shows the distribution of employees at a tech firm by department and whether they work remotely.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Remote} & \text{In-Office} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Engineering} & 85 & 15 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Sales} & 40 & 60 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Marketing} & 30 & 20 & 50 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 155 & 95 & 250 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If an employee who works in-office is selected at random, what is the probability that they work in the Sales department?
questions.explanation: Focus on the 'In-Office' column total (95). The number of Sales employees in this column is 60. The probability is $60/95$, which simplifies to $12/19$.

questions.question:
A library tracked the checkout status of various book types over one weekend.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Checked Out} & \text{Available} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Fiction} & 150 & 50 & 200 \\ \hline \text{Biography} & 40 & 60 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 190 & 110 & 300 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which statement is supported by the data in the table?
options: Half of the total library collection is Fiction.
Biographies are more frequently checked out than Fiction.
Most available books in the library are Biographies.
A random book has a $40\%$ chance of being Fiction.
questions.explanation: Total 'Available' books = 110. The number of available Biographies is 60. Since 60 > 55 (half of 110), most available books are Biographies. Other options are false: Fiction is 200/300 (67%); check-out rate for Fiction (75%) > Biography (40%); random Fiction probability is 200/300 (67%).

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can confidently navigate two-way tables, calculate conditional probabilities, interpret marginal vs. joint frequencies, and apply union logic.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review missed questions and focus on carefully identifying the 'given' condition to select the correct denominator, and remember that 'or' requires subtracting the overlap.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Remember: in two-way tables, the denominator for conditional probability always comes from the row or column total specified in the 'given' clause.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Start by circling the relevant row or column for each question. Label the total, the target group, and the intersection, then translate the prompt into a fraction.
